notes: Official ChatGPT full-context baseline capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Based on your data as of 2026-05-31:

- W-2 employee at Microsoft
- Current 401(k) deferral rate: 8%
- Traditional IRA balance: $0
- One freelance payment received: $1,200
- Seattle resident (no Washington state wage income tax, but federal taxes still apply)

The highest-value 2026 tax items to review are:

1. Check whether your 401(k) deferral rate is high enough

What I see:
- Microsoft paystub gross pay: $2,500 twice per month
- Annualized gross W-2 pay from the data: about $60,000
- Current employee contribution rate: 8%
- Current annual contribution pace: about $4,800/year

Why it matters:
- The employee 401(k) limit is much higher than your current contribution level. The IRS has not necessarily finalized all 2026 limits yet, but the most recently announced limit is $23,500 for employees under age 50 (2025 limit).

Dollar impact:
- At your current pace, you would contribute roughly $4,800.
- If the limit remained $23,500, you would have roughly $18,700 of additional tax-deferred contribution capacity.

Next step:
- Log into your Microsoft benefits portal and confirm:
  - Employer match formula.
  - Whether increasing your deferral percentage would improve your tax situation and savings goals.
- Make sure you contribute at least enough to receive the full employer match, if applicable.

2. Decide whether to make an IRA contribution

What I see:
- Fidelity Traditional IRA balance is currently $0.

Why it matters:
- Depending on your income, filing status, retirement-plan coverage, and tax situation, a Traditional IRA contribution may be deductible, or a Roth IRA contribution may be available.

Limits:
- The IRS has not necessarily released final 2026 IRA limits yet.
- The most recently announced limit is $7,000 per year, plus a $1,000 catch-up contribution for those age 50+.

Important caveat:
- Because you are covered by a workplace retirement plan (your Microsoft 401(k)), deductibility of a Traditional IRA contribution can phase out at higher incomes.
- Roth IRA eligibility also phases out at higher incomes.

Next step:
- Estimate your 2026 adjusted gross income (including any freelance income) before deciding between Traditional and Roth IRA contributions.

3. Account for self-employment tax on the freelance income

What I see:
- Freelance payment: $1,200 from a consulting client.

Why it matters:
- Freelance income is generally subject to:
  - Ordinary federal income tax.
  - Self-employment tax (Social Security and Medicare), unless offset by business deductions.

Approximate impact:
- If $1,200 is your net profit and you have no related business expenses, self-employment tax alone would be roughly $170.
- Federal income tax would be additional.

Next step:
- Keep records of any consulting-related expenses that may be deductible.
- Track whether you receive additional freelance income during 2026.

4. Determine whether quarterly estimated taxes are needed

Why it matters:
- W-2 withholding often covers employee income but not freelance income.
- If consulting work continues, underpayment penalties can apply if too little tax is paid during the year.

What I see:
- Only one freelance payment appears in the data so far.
- It is not yet clear whether this is a one-time project or recurring work.

Next step:
- Estimate total 2026 freelance profit.
- If you expect meaningful additional consulting income, either:
  - Increase W-2 withholding through Microsoft, or
  - Make quarterly estimated tax payments.

Many employees prefer increasing W-2 withholding because it is administratively simpler.

5. Consider a Solo 401(k) only if freelance work becomes significant

Why it matters:
- Self-employment income can create additional retirement-saving opportunities.

Caveat:
- A Solo 401(k) generally only makes sense if consulting income becomes recurring and substantial.
- Contributions depend on net self-employment earnings and interact with your employee contributions at Microsoft.

What I see:
- With only $1,200 of consulting income recorded, this is not yet a major tax-saving opportunity.

Next step:
- Revisit if consulting income grows materially during 2026.

6. Review RSU tax withholding

What I see:
- Microsoft RSU vest deposited: $42,000 on 2026-05-10.

Why it matters:
- RSU vesting is generally taxable as compensation income when vested.
- Large vesting events can sometimes create withholding shortfalls, especially for higher earners.

Next step:
- Review the vesting statement and confirm:
  - Shares withheld for taxes.
  - Federal withholding rate used.
  - Whether projected withholding will be sufficient for your total 2026 tax liability.

Priority order for you:
1. Verify you're receiving the full Microsoft 401(k) match.
2. Review RSU withholding.
3. Estimate total 2026 freelance income and whether estimated taxes are needed.
4. Decide on Traditional vs. Roth IRA contributions.
5. Revisit Solo 401(k) options only if consulting income expands.
